title: The Design of Everyday Things
excerpt: The seminal work on human-centered design, explaining why good design is invisible and bad design frustrates, with foundational concepts like affordances and signifiers.
keyIdeas: Good design is invisible — users only notice it when it fails
Affordances are the relationship between a user and an object's possible actions
Signifiers communicate where actions should take place
Conceptual models allow users to predict how things will behave
The seven stages of action model describes how users interact with products
Human error is usually poor design in disguise
keyTakeaways: Design for error — assume users will make mistakes and make them recoverable
Provide clear signifiers so users know what to do without instruction
Bridge the Gulf of Execution (knowing what to do) and Gulf of Evaluation (knowing what happened)
Use natural mappings between controls and their effects
Constraints prevent errors by limiting what can be done
whoShouldRead: Product designers and UX designers seeking foundational principles
Engineers who build products people interact with
Anyone frustrated by everyday objects that are hard to use
Managers of design and product teams
whoShouldSkip: Readers already familiar with Norman's concepts from other sources
Web designers needing platform-specific UI guidance
People looking for visual design or aesthetics-focused content
